threats.title: AVM-Delegated veNFT Bribe Rewards Permanently Locked Due to Address Mismatch and No-Op setOriginalOwner
threats.hypothesis_description: Bribe rewards for AVM-managed veNFTs are permanently lost. In Bribe.getReward(), ownerOf(tokenId) returns the AutoVotingEscrow instance address (not the manager), so the check `_owner == avm` (comparing against the manager address) is always false. Rewards are sent to the AutoVotingEscrow contract which has no token withdrawal function. Additionally, setOriginalOwner in AutoVotingEscrowManager has an empty function body, so even if the check passed, originalOwner(tokenId) returns address(0).
threats.investigation_rationale: Two independent code defects compound to ensure rewards for all AVM-managed tokens are irrecoverably lost. This affects any user who enables auto-voting.

proof_of_signal.pattern: ownerOf returns AutoVotingEscrow address, compared against avm (manager address); setOriginalOwner has empty body

preconditions: User has enabled auto-voting for a veNFT
veNFT has been voted to earn bribe rewards
gaugeManager calls getReward for the tokenId

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Address mismatch: ownerOf returns AVE address != avm (manager); setOriginalOwner is a no-op
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Bribe.avm = AutoVotingEscrowManager address, but tokens are held by individual AutoVotingEscrow instances. safeTransfer sends rewards to AutoVotingEscrow which has no recovery function
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No iteration over AVM instances to check ownership, no token recovery in AutoVotingEscrow

analysis_questions: Can rewards accumulate in the AutoVotingEscrow contract indefinitely?
Is there any indirect mechanism to recover tokens from AutoVotingEscrow?
Does the double failure (address mismatch + no-op) affect all AVM users or only specific scenarios?
validation_requirements: Trace getReward flow for AVM-managed tokenId end-to-end
Verify AutoVotingEscrow has no ERC20 withdrawal function
Confirm setOriginalOwner function body is empty

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Any user who calls enableAutoVoting will have all future bribe rewards permanently locked in the AutoVotingEscrow contract. No adversarial action needed - this is a systematic loss affecting all AVM users.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: 100% of bribe rewards for all AVM-delegated veNFTs permanently lost
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None without contract upgrade or emergency recovery mechanism

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for AVM bribe reward claiming flow

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Any time bribe rewards are claimed
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by checking AutoVotingEscrow token balances

threats.title: Governance Delegation Checkpoint Wipe on Any Token Transfer via moveTokenDelegates ownerOf Bug
threats.hypothesis_description: In VotingDelegationLib.moveTokenDelegates(), when rebuilding the source delegate's checkpoint, the function filters tokens using `ownerOfFn(tId) == srcRep`. For delegated tokens (where the owner delegated voting power to srcRep), ownerOfFn returns the original owner's address, not srcRep. This causes ALL delegated-from-others tokens to be dropped from srcRep's checkpoint on any single token transfer, burn, or merge operation involving srcRep's delegators.
threats.investigation_rationale: The filter condition should be `tId != _tokenId` (exclude only the transferred token) instead of `ownerOfFn(tId) == srcRep` (keep only tokens owned by the delegate). This architectural error means governance voting power can be silently wiped.

proof_of_signal.pattern: ownerOfFn(tId) == srcRep filters out all tokens delegated by other owners to srcRep

preconditions: Multiple users have delegated their veNFTs to a common delegate
Any token transfer, merge, split, or burn involving a token whose owner uses that delegate triggers the wipe

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Source checkpoint rebuilt by keeping only tokens where ownerOf(tId) == srcRep, dropping all externally delegated tokens
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: After any _transferFrom from a delegator, their delegate loses ALL other delegated voting power in getVotes/getPastVotes
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should filter by tId != _tokenId instead of ownerOf check

analysis_questions: Does the same-block checkpoint optimization path have the same bug?
How does _moveAllDelegates handle this differently?
Can an attacker intentionally trigger this to zero out a governance delegate's voting power?
validation_requirements: Create test: A delegates to C, B delegates to C. A transfers token. Verify C's getVotes includes B's tokens.
Verify the non-checkpoint-in-new-block path has the same vulnerability
Assess impact on governance proposals and quorum

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker identifies a prominent delegate with significant delegated voting power. Attacker delegates a minimal veNFT to the same delegate, then transfers that veNFT. This triggers moveTokenDelegates which rebuilds the delegate's checkpoint, dropping ALL other delegated tokens. The delegate's governance voting power is zeroed out.
prerequisites: Target delegate has tokens delegated from multiple users
Attacker can create and transfer a veNFT

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Governance manipulation enabling malicious proposals to pass
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users who delegate voting power
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Delegation must be re-established by each delegator individually

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for multi-delegator delegation checkpoint consistency after transfers

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing getVotes before/after transfer

threats.title: RewardsDistributor.claim_many Sends Expired AVM Lock Rewards to AutoVotingEscrow Contract
threats.hypothesis_description: claim_many() does not check AVM ownership for expired locks, unlike claim() which resolves via avm.getOriginalOwner(). For AVM-managed tokens with expired non-permanent locks, claim_many sends rewards directly to ownerOf(tokenId) which is the AutoVotingEscrow contract. These tokens are permanently trapped.
threats.investigation_rationale: The single claim() function has AVM-aware routing, but claim_many() bypasses this entirely, creating an inconsistency where the same operation produces different (and incorrect) results when batched.

proof_of_signal.pattern: claim_many sends to ownerOf without AVM check; claim at lines 105-123 has AVM resolution

preconditions: veNFT is in AVM (auto-voting enabled)
Lock has expired (end < block.timestamp) and is not permanent
claim_many is called (by anyone) for this tokenId

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Missing AVM ownership resolution in claim_many vs claim
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: IERC20(token).transfer(_nftOwner, amount) where _nftOwner = AutoVotingEscrow address with no withdrawal
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No avm.tokenIdToAVMId check or avm.getOriginalOwner resolution in claim_many

analysis_questions: Can anyone call claim_many for arbitrary tokenIds to trigger this?
Are there guard mechanisms preventing expired locks from remaining in AVM?
What percentage of AVM locks might realistically expire?
validation_requirements: Verify claim_many code path omits AVM check
Test with AVM-managed expired lock and confirm reward destination
Check if AutoVotingEscrow has any token recovery mechanism

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Anyone calls claim_many with AVM-managed tokenIds that have expired locks. Rebase rewards are sent to AutoVotingEscrow contracts which cannot forward them. Users lose accumulated rebase rewards.
prerequisites: AVM-managed veNFTs with expired locks exist

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of rebase rewards for AVM users with expired locks
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: AVM users who let locks expire

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test comparing claim vs claim_many for AVM tokens

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring token transfers to AutoVotingEscrow addresses

threats.title: PermissionsRegistry.removeRole Corrupts _addressToRoles Using Wrong Source Array for Swap-and-Pop
threats.hypothesis_description: In removeRole(), when cleaning up _addressToRoles for affected users, the code uses `_roles[_roles.length - 1]` (the last element of the GLOBAL roles array) instead of `_addressToRoles[rta[i]][_addressToRoles[rta[i]].length - 1]` (the last element of the user's role array) for the swap-and-pop operation. This corrupts users' role lists with unrelated role entries.
threats.investigation_rationale: The bug is in the swap-and-pop implementation which reads from the wrong array. Additionally, the inner loop doesn't break after pop, potentially causing further corruption. This code runs during normal admin operations (removeRole is an intended admin function).
preconditions: blackMultisig calls removeRole for an existing role
At least one address has that role assigned

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Wrong source array in swap-and-pop: _roles instead of _addressToRoles[rta[i]]
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: After removeRole, affected addresses have corrupted role entries in _addressToRoles, potentially affecting subsequent removeRoleFrom operations
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No break after pop; wrong array reference

analysis_questions: Does the corruption in _addressToRoles affect hasRole checks?
Can subsequent removeRoleFrom calls behave incorrectly due to corrupted data?
Does the missing break after pop cause additional issues?
validation_requirements: Test removeRole with multiple addresses assigned, verify _addressToRoles integrity
Verify hasRole mapping is correctly updated (it uses a separate mapping)
Test removeRoleFrom after removeRole on affected addresses

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Admin calls removeRole('GAUGE_ADMIN'). For each address that had GAUGE_ADMIN, their _addressToRoles entry is replaced with the last element of global _roles (e.g., 'EPOCH_MANAGER'). The addressToRole view returns incorrect data, and subsequent removeRoleFrom operations may behave incorrectly or revert.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Moderate - role tracking corruption could prevent proper role management
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All addresses that had the removed role
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires manual cleanup of role assignments

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for removeRole with multiple assigned addresses

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by calling addressToRole() after removeRole

threats.title: delegateBySig Replay After Direct delegate() Call Due to Nonce Not Incrementing
threats.hypothesis_description: The delegate() function does not increment nonces[msg.sender], so previously signed delegateBySig messages remain valid after the signer changes their delegation via delegate(). An attacker holding a previously signed delegation message can submit it after the signer calls delegate() to override the new delegation.
threats.investigation_rationale: Standard EIP-712 delegation patterns require nonce invalidation on any delegation change. The delegate() function bypasses nonce increment, leaving old signatures perpetually valid until used.

proof_of_signal.pattern: delegate() calls _delegate() without incrementing nonces[msg.sender]; delegateBySig at line 619 does increment nonces[signatory]

preconditions: User has previously signed a delegateBySig message to an attacker
User subsequently calls delegate() to change delegation
Attacker still holds the old signed message

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: delegate() does not increment nonces; delegateBySig checks nonces[signatory]++
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Old signatures remain valid after direct delegation change, allowing attacker to override user's current delegation

analysis_questions: How commonly is delegateBySig used in the protocol ecosystem?
Can the expiry parameter mitigate this in practice?
Does the lack of s-value range check introduce additional malleability risks?
validation_requirements: Create test: Sign delegation, call delegate() directly, submit old signature
Verify nonce state after delegate() vs delegateBySig
Assess governance impact of delegation hijacking

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Alice signs delegation to Charlie (nonce=0). Alice changes mind, calls delegate(Bob). Nonce stays 0. Charlie submits the old signature with nonce=0, which validates, redirecting Alice's voting power back to Charlie without consent.
prerequisites: Victim previously signed a delegation message

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Governance manipulation through stolen voting power delegation
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users who have ever used delegateBySig and later changed delegation
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: User must call delegateBySig (not delegate) to increment nonce

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for nonce consistency between delegate and delegateBySig

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Any time after victim changes delegation

threats.title: Bribe earned() Unbounded Epoch Iteration with WEEK=1800 Causes DoS on Reward Claims
threats.hypothesis_description: The earned() function iterates over all epochs since lastEarn with no iteration cap. With WEEK=1800 seconds (30 minutes), even 7 real days creates 336 epochs. Each iteration reads multiple storage slots from checkpoints and supply. This exceeds block gas limits, permanently locking bribe rewards for users who haven't claimed recently.
threats.investigation_rationale: BlackTimeLibrary.WEEK = 1800 seconds means epochs accumulate ~48x faster than a standard weekly epoch. The earned() loop has no cap, unlike RewardsDistributor._claim which caps at 50 iterations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: for loop iterating numEpochs with no upper bound; WEEK=1800 at BlackTimeLibrary.sol:5

preconditions: User has not claimed bribe rewards for more than a few days
WEEK=1800 seconds (confirmed in BlackTimeLibrary)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unbounded loop: numEpochs = (epochStart(block.timestamp) - _currTs) / WEEK; no cap on iterations
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: With WEEK=1800, 7 days = 336 iterations, each with 2+ SLOAD operations = potential OOG
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No iteration cap like RewardsDistributor's 50-week limit

analysis_questions: What is the gas cost per iteration of the earned loop?
At what number of epochs does this reliably exceed block gas limit?
Can a partial claim mechanism be implemented to mitigate?
validation_requirements: Gas benchmark earned() with increasing epoch counts
Determine exact threshold where OOG occurs on target chain
Verify that lastEarn cannot be updated without completing the full loop

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A user who voted and has not claimed bribe rewards for more than a few days finds that getReward reverts due to OOG. Their bribe rewards are permanently locked since lastEarn is only updated inside getReward, creating a dead-end state.
prerequisites: User has unclaimed bribe rewards spanning multiple days

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent loss of bribe rewards for affected users
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any voter who doesn't claim frequently enough

threats.test_coverage_gap: No gas benchmark test for earned() with many epochs

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Precise gas per iteration measurement needed

threats.title: VotingEscrow _checkpoint Loop Covers Only 5.3 Days with WEEK=1800, Breaking Global Voting Power
threats.hypothesis_description: The _checkpoint function iterates at most 255 times with WEEK=1800 second increments, covering only 255*1800=459,000 seconds (~5.3 days). If no checkpoint occurs for longer, the global point_history becomes stale, causing incorrect totalSupply calculations that affect all reward distributions and governance votes.
threats.investigation_rationale: The 255-iteration limit was designed for WEEK=604800 (7 days), giving ~4.9 years coverage. With WEEK=1800, coverage shrinks to 5.3 days, making staleness realistic during low-activity periods.

proof_of_signal.pattern: for (uint i = 0; i < 255; ++i) { t_i += WEEK; } with WEEK=1800

preconditions: No user interaction triggers _checkpoint for more than 5.3 days
WEEK=1800 seconds

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: 255 iterations * 1800 seconds = 459000 seconds max catch-up; comment says 'Hopefully it won't happen... in 5 years'
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Global slope_changes not applied past 5.3 days, causing totalSupply to be incorrect
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No mechanism to force periodic checkpoints; no higher iteration limit

analysis_questions: How likely is a 5.3-day gap in protocol activity on Avalanche?
What is the magnitude of totalSupply error after a missed checkpoint window?
Can multiple sequential checkpoint calls catch up?
validation_requirements: Simulate 6+ days without checkpoint, verify totalSupply accuracy
Test if sequential checkpoint() calls can recover
Quantify error magnitude in reward distribution

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: During a low-activity period exceeding 5.3 days, the global point_history becomes stale. An attacker then triggers a checkpoint, but it only catches up partially. totalSupply is incorrect, inflating or deflating users' share of rebase rewards and enabling governance quorum manipulation.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Incorrect reward distributions for all users; potential governance manipulation
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Multiple checkpoint calls may eventually catch up, but permanent error in point_history

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for checkpoint behavior with large time gaps relative to WEEK

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Compare totalSupply vs sum of individual balances after gap

threats.title: SMNFT_BONUS Change Breaks totalSupply vs Individual Balance Invariant
threats.hypothesis_description: When team calls setSmNFTBonus, the global point's smNFTBonus is recalculated using calculate_sm_nft_bonus(smNFTBalance) with the NEW rate at every _checkpoint, but individual smNFT token checkpoints retain their OLD stored smNFTBonus values until individually re-checkpointed. This breaks the invariant that totalSupply == sum(balanceOfNFT) and causes incorrect reward distributions.
threats.investigation_rationale: In _checkpoint, `last_point.smNFTBonus = calculate_sm_nft_bonus(smNFTBalance)` uses the current SMNFT_BONUS. But each token's user_point_history stores smNFTBonus from creation time. After a bonus change, the global and individual views diverge.

proof_of_signal.pattern: last_point.smNFTBonus = calculate_sm_nft_bonus(smNFTBalance) uses current SMNFT_BONUS; individual checkpoints store fixed values

preconditions: smNFTs exist in the system
Team calls setSmNFTBonus to change the bonus rate

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Global checkpoint uses live calculate_sm_nft_bonus; individual checkpoints store historical values
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: If bonus increases: totalSupply inflated relative to sum of individual balances. If decreases: totalSupply deflated. Both cause incorrect reward share calculations.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No migration mechanism to update existing smNFT checkpoints on bonus change

analysis_questions: How large is the divergence for realistic bonus changes?
Does the divergence self-correct when individual tokens are checkpointed?
Does RewardsDistributor use totalSupplyAtT for reward calculation?
validation_requirements: Create smNFTs, change bonus, compare totalSupply vs sum of balanceOfNFT
Verify RewardsDistributor._claim uses totalSupplyAtT in share calculation
Quantify reward distribution error

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: After team increases SMNFT_BONUS, totalSupply is inflated by the bonus increase on smNFTBalance but individual smNFT balances remain unchanged until re-checkpointed. Normal lock holders receive less than their fair share of rebase rewards because supply denominator is artificially high.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Incorrect rebase reward distribution to all users
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All veNFT holders (diluted if bonus increases)
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Partially recoverable by individually checkpointing all smNFTs

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for SMNFT_BONUS change impact on existing locks

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Compare totalSupply vs sum of individual balanceOfNFT

threats.title: VoterV3 voted Flag Stuck True When All Voted Gauges Are Killed, Blocking Token Transfers
threats.hypothesis_description: When poke() or vote() is called and all previously voted gauges are killed, _vote() calls _reset() (clearing weights) but only calls IVotingEscrow(_ve).voting(_tokenId) when _usedWeight > 0. Since all gauges are dead, _usedWeight = 0 and voting() is not called. However, the voted flag remains true from the previous vote, preventing transfers and withdrawals until the user calls reset() in the next epoch.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _vote function sets voted=true only when _usedWeight > 0, but doesn't set it to false when _usedWeight = 0. Since poke() doesn't have onlyNewEpoch modifier restrictions, this state can be reached within the current epoch.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (_usedWeight > 0) IVotingEscrow(_ve).voting(_tokenId); — voted flag not cleared when _usedWeight == 0

preconditions: User has active votes on gauges
All voted gauges are killed
poke() is called (e.g., via deposit_for or increase_amount)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: voted flag only set to true when _usedWeight > 0; abstain only called in explicit reset()
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: After poke with all dead gauges: usedWeights=0 but voted=true. Token cannot be transferred or withdrawn.

analysis_questions: Can this be triggered via deposit_for which calls poke when voted?
How long is the user locked (until next epoch's reset window)?
Can this be weaponized to prevent emergency withdrawals?
validation_requirements: Test: vote for gauge, kill gauge, call deposit_for, verify transfer blocked
Verify reset() in next epoch properly clears voted flag
Check if there's any other way to clear voted flag

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A governance actor kills all gauges that a target user has voted for. When poke is triggered (via deposit_for or increase_amount), the user's voted flag stays true but usedWeights becomes 0. The user cannot transfer or withdraw their veNFT until the next epoch when they can call reset().

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Temporary lock of user funds (one epoch duration)
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Automatic recovery next epoch via reset()

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for gauge killing impact on voted flag consistency

threats.title: AutoVotingEscrow.lockOwner Returns Incorrect Owner for Non-Existent or Released Token IDs
threats.hypothesis_description: lockOwner() uses tokenIdIndex mapping which defaults to 0 for non-existent tokenIds. If locks array is non-empty, lockOwner returns locks[0].owner for any tokenId not in the AVM, including released tokens whose index was deleted (reset to 0). The require check `index < locks.length` passes when locks is non-empty.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function lacks a validation that locks[index].tokenId == _tokenId, meaning the returned owner may belong to a completely different token. While the manager's tokenIdToAVMId check provides a guard, any direct call to lockOwner bypasses this.

proof_of_signal.pattern: tokenIdIndex defaults to 0; no validation that locks[index].tokenId == _tokenId

preconditions: At least one lock exists in the AutoVotingEscrow
lockOwner is called with a tokenId that was never added or was previously released

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: tokenIdIndex[_tokenId] defaults to 0; returns locks[0].owner regardless of actual tokenId
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: getOriginalOwner calls lockOwner, potentially returning wrong owner for reward routing

analysis_questions: Does tokenIdToAVMId in the manager fully protect against this in all call paths?
Can getOriginalOwner return wrong owner and cause reward misrouting?
Are there external contracts that call lockOwner directly?
validation_requirements: Add token to AVM, release it, verify lockOwner behavior with released tokenId
Test getOriginalOwner for non-AVM tokenIds
Check all callers of lockOwner across the codebase

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If any external integration calls lockOwner directly (bypassing tokenIdToAVMId), it receives the wrong owner for non-existent tokenIds, potentially enabling reward theft or authorization bypass.
prerequisites: At least one lock in the AutoVotingEscrow
Direct call to lockOwner with invalid tokenId

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Potential reward misrouting to wrong address
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users whose tokens share an AVM with the victim

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for lockOwner with non-existent or released tokenIds

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Compare lockOwner output with actual lock data

threats.title: BlackClaims recoverERC20 Can Drain Active Season Reward Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: The recoverERC20() function allows the owner to withdraw ANY ERC20 token, including the reward token (BLACK), from the contract. During an active claiming season, calling recoverERC20(address(token)) would drain the contract's reward balance, causing subsequent claimAndStakeReward calls to fail due to insufficient balance, without updating remaining_reward_amount.
threats.investigation_rationale: Unlike emergencyRecoverERC20 in Bribe.sol which is documented as dangerous, BlackClaims.recoverERC20 has no exclusion of the reward token and no accounting adjustment. The contract's dev comment suggests it's for recovering accidentally sent tokens, but it can drain active season funds.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No check that tokenAddress_ != address(token); transfers full balance to msg.sender

preconditions: Owner calls recoverERC20 with the reward token address
Season is finalized with remaining_reward_amount > 0

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No exclusion of reward token from recovery; transfers full balance without accounting update
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: remaining_reward_amount not updated, causing _preClaim to succeed but token.transfer to fail
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing require(tokenAddress_ != address(token)) or accounting adjustment

analysis_questions: Is this intentional as an emergency mechanism?
Does the owner role have other mechanisms to drain funds?
Can remaining_reward_amount underflow after partial recovery?
validation_requirements: Test recoverERC20 with reward token during active season
Verify claimAndStakeReward fails after recovery
Check if revokeUnclaimedReward is the intended recovery path

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Compromised or malicious owner calls recoverERC20(address(token)) during active claiming season, draining all BLACK tokens. Users who haven't claimed yet find claimAndStakeReward reverts on token transfer, permanently losing their seasonal rewards.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of all unclaimed seasonal rewards
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users with unclaimed season rewards
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Owner could return tokens, but no on-chain enforcement

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for recoverERC20 with reward token during active season
